policy_statement: Austin will achieve net-zero community-wide greenhouse gas emissions by 2040.
mechanism_description: Sets a community-wide net-zero emissions target that drives planning and actions across sectors to reduce greenhouse gas emissions over time.
typology_evidence_quote: "Austin will achieve net-zero community-wide greenhouse gas emissions by 2040."
primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance alignment → cross-sector measures implemented → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: The jurisdiction sets an overarching, quantitative GHG target (e.g., % reduction by a year, carbon neutrality) and aligns planning, budgeting, accountability, and agency mandates to it.  
2) Mediating process: The target creates a governance signal that induces adoption of sector-specific policies/investments (efficiency, electrification, clean power, mode shift, methane controls), shifting technology and behavior and reducing fossil fuel combustion and non-CO₂ sources.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced aggregate territorial/consumption-based (as defined) GHG emissions over the target period, lowering cumulative emissions and associated warming.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is reducing net GHG emissions across the jurisdiction by setting a binding/operative emissions-reduction trajectory that drives sectoral mitigation actions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Target-setting is designed to catalyze and coordinate actions whose main physical outcome is lower GHG emissions; the emissions decrease is the direct climate-relevant endpoint.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can incidentally improve resource efficiency (energy/material productivity) or have co-benefits for resilience and air quality, those are secondary; the mechanism’s unifying purpose across all instances is emissions reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets may be framed as “carbon neutrality” via offsets or include adaptation language, but the dominant pathway remains mitigation because the metric of success is net GHG reduction (absolute cuts and/or compensated emissions). Targets scoped to specific operations (e.g., airports/ports) are still mitigation targets even if not fully “economy-wide” in practice.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that uses this mechanism (setting an overarching GHG target to drive subsequent actions) shares the same primary mitigation pathway—governance signal → implementation → emissions reductions—regardless of sectoral mix or jurisdiction.  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any finer subtype (e.g., “governance/planning,” “net-zero with offsets,” “sectoral operations target”) should be assigned later based on policy-level evidence and scope details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets achieved primarily through offsets/removals (vs. direct reductions) can shift the proximate mechanism toward “↑ carbon sinks/negative emissions,” but the primary category remains Mitigation because the defining outcome is lower net GHG. Scope ambiguity (economy-wide vs. organizational boundary like airport/port) affects specificity but not the dominant mitigation pathway.
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements; possible resilience co-benefits via electrification/efficiency (heat, drought/water stress), but not stated.
instance_edge_case_notes: Not specified whether the target is legally binding, includes interim targets/carbon budgets, or defines accounting scope (territorial vs consumption-based, offsets). No explicit adaptation elements despite local hazards.
